commentBody: Thanks for a nice one, Kameron. It should make some people feel better.
Toyota Tacoma … no…Tercel… no…Tundra!
Got it but fursona almost did me in. Good puzzle
My FURSONA is a POLLIWOG who hangs out in the HOTELPOOL.
When I see Kameron Austin Collins on a Saturday, I roll up my sleeves for a challenge. But today’s puzzle, while a nice construction, was kind of a breeze.

Good puzzle today, clever cluing and not too many obscure proper noun regions.  I love that they got WHATABOUTISM in.  I'd never heard the term FURSONA but it was 'Figure-outable' with a few letters.

Toughest region for me was northeast.  First having never heard of the movie HUD.  But also I think "Come back" is not a good clue for RECUR and I first had AGAST instead of AGAPE, which made it really tough to figure out READS and UPTURN.

38A/D I had COMETO and CURL… Needless to say, the SE kept me out of it for a long while. 

Good puzzle today! Center section was a good run!
Shouldn’t 5 down be plural?
Tasty. Challenging without being maddening. I’ll sleep well. Could have been tougher, yet, still oddly satisfying. 😊

Inner tube was a good one.  Learned a lot tonight. 

Thx

A thought: It may be that we OGs are just getting better at combining memory, intuition and brilliance to solve faster than we did 5 or 10 years ago.  

Sometimes I can just hear the answer in my head and I don’t really know where they’re coming from. That didn’t used to happen as much.

Great to see this Kameron Austin Collins puzzle so soon after January's, considering we had that KAC drought. Still, I expect to wrestle with Mr. Collins' puzzles for 30-60 minutes.

That being said, I enjoyed every one of the (substantially fewer) minutes I spent on today's puzzle. And I learned a few new terms, as I expected to after seeing today's byline. Still a huge fan.

On behalf of those of us who don’t have 1969 and 1979 trivia at the tip of our tongue to anchor the middle of this puzzle, but who regularly solve Saturday puzzles, please don’t start any nonsense about how that one was too easy. That was hard.
This was a fun puzzle. Especially compared to last Saturday.

I thoroughly enjoyed this. Nothing too flashy but some fun entries like WHATABOUTISM and FURSONA and BOOKTOK and BLOTTY. For me this was about average Saturday difficulty and that’s fine. SESAMESTREET was great trivia and I needed a few crosses for it. I should have known PAULSIMON but was thinking it was a movie—good ambiguous clueing. Maybe MOONRAKER should have been a gimme since I’ve seen every Bond movie two or three times but somehow it wasn’t. And I had BELTLOOPS before BELTLINES and DOGHAIR before CATHAIR. 

Someone please tell me they at least tried BRA for “bosom buddy”. And speaking of bosom buddies I’ve been a pediatrician for 35 years and never ever heard anyone call a baby bottle nipple a TEAT. Apparently a thing—maybe it’s regional? Anyone else?

"Why'd you give me this six-sided cube?"
"Just roll with it!"

(But then the game ended. It was dice while it lasted.)

My thoughts on the puzzle:

I was all at sea for what felt like a lo-o-o-ng time. TIL FURSONA, WHATABOUTISM, LETT and TWOHEADED. And I'm not (like really *really* not) a fan of BLOTTY. 
But in the end, still got the gold star in a reasonable time with reasonable effort. A much better puzzle than recent experiences have led me to expect.
Never heard of HUD.  Had me challenged in the NE.  I thought was Paul Newman ever Ben HUR?  Nah, that was that other guy.

I love the picture of the tree kangaroo at the top of the column. Woodland Park Zoo, here in Seattle, has long partnered with the people of Papua New Guinea to save the endangered Matschie’s Tree Kangaroo. On May 1st, a new building will be opening that will allow zoo guests to see them. Here’s some info: ITree Kangaroo Conservation Program: https://zoo.org/tkcp/ This 9 minute video about the program, including the coffee the people there grow to sell here: https://youtu.be/iFhYdYjmPWY Want to see video shot by a tree kangaroo? There’s a bit in this 10 minute video: https://youtu.be/9cXdtS8qNnc

For those who didn’t know LETT I owe that one to Ella Fitzgerald and Cole Porter in one of my favorite lyrics:

And that's why birds do it, bees do it
Even educated fleas do it
Let's do it, let's fall in love
In Spain the best upper sets do it
Lithuanians and Letts do it
Let's do it, let's fall in love
The Dutch in old Amsterdam do it
Not to mention the Finns
Folks in Siam do it--
Think of Siamese twins
Some Argentines, without means, do it
People say in Boston even beans do it
Let's do it, let's fall in love

Welcome back, KAC! What a treat it is to have you back so soon. Off the bat, I was taken by the beauty of the grid design which, now I know, we have Natan Last to thank for. As for the puzzle itself, it showcases KAC's amazing talent for construction, and I can almost feel the love he puts into it coming through. As always, thank you! ............................................... For a sedate Friday night groove, just kick back with Teddy Pendergrass' s Love TKO - https://youtu.be/Pb4j2UqkOJ0?si=SAsAXuUALHC19T4p For a get-up-and-go Saturday, be sure to ROLL WITH IT, with Steve Winwood - https://youtu.be/u_vkKozA8OI?si=C_oAxAiSWSZnRf41 A great weekend to all!

Anyone heard from SBK recently?

BOLO for SBK.

Really like the shout to Paul Simon and "The Boxer". What a great song, gorgeous lyrics: "Asking only workman's wages I come looking for a job But I get no offers. Just a come-on from the whοres on Seventh Avenue. I do declare there were times when I was so lonesome I took some comfort there." Powerful and succinct. Another amazing song, maybe less well known, that brings a tear to my eye every time, more and more as time goes on--American Tune. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AE3kKUEY5WU

Anyone else notice that GoldenEye has the same number of letters as MOONRAKER?  

Anyone else never heard of a FURSONA before? (Wild stab fixed that one.)

Anyone else misspell Eustac(h)ian and get saved—down by LEY?

Listen, this was a terrific puzzle and I had a terrific time solving it.  It was a beauty, Kameron, and if a seem a bit hysterical it's not because of the crossword—not a single cross word for you.  It's that I filled in every single box, and  n o t h i n g   h a p p e n e d !  No congrats, no try-again-dear—nada.  Combed it for blanks (none), tried again, took out words and put them back, and finally, finally reloaded the page and got the almost-there message.  Found the error, calmed down, sort of (adrenaline still pretty high) and came here.

It was a killer of a puzzle and I loved it.  No padding, no garbage, just solid and fun the whole way through.

Thank you!!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: That'll do, Saturday.
A good Saturday for me is one where I have like…one entry on my first pass, and then things fall into place from there. This was a good Saturday.

POLLIWOGS!!!!!!!! Loved the clue, made me smile!

Go Gunners!
Really wanted 34A to be Bra :D
Fun puzzle! In the TIL category, I learned 'Fursona'

I had bullfrOGS for POLLIWOGS, TacomA for TUNDRA, vat for WOK, pal for BFF.

I didn’t have anything for FURSONA, BOOKTOK, or BLOTTY because I’m not sure these are words that existed before tonight. 

If anyone starts complaining about “too easy”, I may have to throw things. (Either way, I enjoyed the challenge.)

You gotta love it when an entry that has appeared 188 times gets a fresh and clever clue. 

[Many an “SVU” extra] 

EMT

Good one!
As a furry, finding FURSONA in the word list felt like seeing your teacher at a concert. It’s cool to see, though!

Wow this was tough. The top left corner really got me bad. A great Saturday overall, tough with good wordplay and surprisingly very less lame crossword glue. The best part was when I cracked SESAME STREET and I went all "Oooooooohhhhhhh! Oscar!" 😂
What a fun Saturday! Very satisfying!
So it's not FALLOPIAN after all.

I don’t normally comment, but after having done these puzzles every day for years, it is impossible not he become annoyed that difficulty has turned from challenging vocabulary and wordplay to, “do you consume the same pop culture and hobbies as the constructor.” It is lazy, and uninteresting and a cop out. It’s not a bad puzzle, but this trend has really sapped the fun and challenge from these puzzles.

TIL that Sixty Minutes (maybe an Oscar for short form doc?) and Sesame Street have the same number of letters, as do Tercel and Tundra (surely Tercel was earlier though).  The lack of abbv. Indicator for BFF threw me off for far longer than it should have.  Fun Saturday overall.
Why has the app changed to open word play in the app. Makes toggling between blog & puzzle a pain. No dark option either. Annoying. Make it optional.
Toughie
Shout out to the sub-editor who wrote the caption for the picture of the (?) marsupial at the top of the Wordplay column. It’s so clever and witty.

A kinder, gentler KAK today. I kind of prefer the crueler, tougher KAK, but a fun puzzle anyway. For a more challenging (for me, anyway) option, today's Stumper by David P. Williams took me almost twice as long. https://brainsonly.com/global/netword/cwd/#/s/260314

Perfect kind of hard Saturday for me. Loved my DOH/aha moment when I finally saw Sesame Street! Especially liked intestine for "inner tube". Smiled when I changed 'Persona' to 'Fursona' with the entry of sour!😊

Well that was interesting. I was convinced there was a rebus in there as 5A was clearly either the Younger or the Elder (father and son both held the PM role). In that mode I convinced myself that it also had to be Live and Let Die at 31A. I TWISTed myself in knots trying to make it fit. Silly me.

Once I shook off the rebus obsession things fell much more smoothly. Laughed like a drain once I got the Oscar ref with SESAME STREET. It’s weird what you soak up by osmosis; that programme was never a feature in our household  (don’t get me started on zee/zed when you’re teaching children to speak!) and yet I’m completely aware of Big Bird, Elmo, Cookie Monster et al.

TIL POLLIWOG. I admit to a frisson of disquiet when I filled the clue; it’s one letter away from a name now considered completely unacceptable here. I high tailed it to Google to find out what it meant. Phew.

Overall a great Saturday grid. Thank you Mr Collins.

SPOILER ALERT - I had a different amphibian name at 37A until I figured out POLLIWOG, but stumbled upon my first impression when the term became a pangram in today's Spelling Bee. Coincidence? Yeah, probably.

This is a shimmering box in the Crosslandia aisle.  Filled with personality and you-don’t-know-what-you’re-going-to-turn-up-next.

ACOLYTE!  POLLIWOGS!  WHATABOUTISM!  FURSONA!  GOD FEARING!  BLOTTY!

The center vibrates with freshness.  That five-answer stagger stack? – one once-before-in-the-Times answer, three twice-befores, and once thrice before.  Furthermore, it is crossed by a debut and three once-befores.

Are you kidding me?

The cluing variety you want on Saturday.  Is [Promenade] a noun or verb?  Misdirects – [Tanning target], [Sporting blades], [Top option].  Humor -- [They croak as soon as they grow up].

Hidden beneath the flash is stellar construction, the KAC hallmark that rarely gets mentioned in the comments.  We take it for granted.  But his puzzles are *always* squeaky clean.

Cameron, I knew I was in for a treat when “mottled” and “flutters” appeared in NW clues, and once again, the rest of the box delivered.  Thank you, and more please!
That was fun and accessible. The right level of difficulty IMHO.
FURSONA in the NYT crossword. Truly, an historic day.
Gotta say it’s been a 2026 feast to have travelled to KACistan twice before spring.

Fee, fi, fo…fun!
Amirite?

Woke up extra brilliant today,
and was humming “The Boxer”
as I tried to think of the non-existent
movie with that name 
and who might have written it.

But once I ran through *every* Oscar
in my noodle, 
it all fell together 
like a flock of birds,
taking flight in crazy, messy mayhem
then suddenly
*whooosh*
they are all flying in perfect unison.

[happy sigh]
Brilliant!

Whew. Tough one for me, with more than a couple of completely unfamiliar answers (BOOKTOK, FURSONA and others). Had to look some things up to get through it. Oh well. A couple of puzzle finds today - the first one just a cute Tuesday from September 5, 2000 by Chris Sallade. Theme answers in that one (all straightforwardly clued): GINGERROGERS SAGEADVICE CURRYFAVOR SESAMESTREET And the reveal - a pair of answers: 9a: "With 69-across, locales for this puzzles theme." SPICE 69a: RACKS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/5/2000&g=58&d=A I'll put the other puzzle in a reply. ....

Something I learned just today is that anyone here from Wiltshire County, England should be either offended or proud as they reflect on seeing MOONRAKER in the puzzle. 


I found this puzzle a blast from start to finish - and I don’t think that’s only because too much acolyte has made me blotty.
Gullah Gullah Island is also where I learned about POLLIWOGs!

Pretty amazing themeless puzzle, just packed with interesting entries.

Got it solved without help, but took just over thirty minutes. Having TEN (as in 10 out of 10) rather than TEE for "Top option"  was unfortunate, and keep me from seeing SESAMESTREET for much too long. TERCEL before TUNDRA and HOTSPRING before HOTELPOOL also made the SE corner slow going today. 

Didn't know BOOKTOK or FURSONA, but both were easily gotten with help from the clues and crosses.

Seemed like an excellent Saturday puzzle to me---a Puzzle of the Year contender given the quality of the many long entries alone.
AWESOME PUZZLE! Thank you. More Saturdays like this, please.
KAC doesn't just tok the tok, he woks the wok!

And side note-

when the latest update to the app
was too fancy for my old rickety iPad,
I wept as I busted out my laptop
for my morning solve.

Hubby surprised me yesterday 
with a shiny new iPad.

Holy smokes. 
The solving experience is
so different and better in every way.

How some solve on their phones
seems utterly impossible to me.


This concludes today’s edition of
“CC and her First World Problems.
Well, wasn't that fun?
Fursona? Really?

Kameron has already taken my scalp via a previous puzzle; he'll have to find a substitute.  I am stumped at the criss-crossing of the POLLYWOG (POLLiWOG?) and the Shakespeare quote (SIN?) and the "SVU" extra (EXT would break all the rules.)  My copybook is BLOTTY...if that's a word.

Other than that, I enjoyed the challenge of this puzzle.  CAT HAIR is part of our life; we just ROLL WITH IT (you know, those little sticky-pick-up tools.)  

Fave (all new!) entry:  FURSONA.
That, and the POLLYWOG....(I'm humming "The Golliwog's Cake-walk"....)

OK, so I've been playing around in the archives, and I hadn't had coffee...so I totally discounted the possibility of BFF for 34A. LOL Took a while to get rid of "pal". 
Anyone else misconstrue 37A and start with "bullfrogs"? I already had the OG. 
Don't worry...I'm drinking my coffee now.
Today’s puzzle hit the Goldilocks spot - not too hot, not too cold, _just right_. Now let me get a move on before the bears return!

One more puzzle find, inspired by today's date. A Wednesday from March 14 (!) 2012 by David Sarpola. First - four answers, straightforwardly clued: THREETOED POINTSPREAD ONEMANSHOWS FOURSCORE And then the very last across answer in the bottom right corner: "Mid-March celebration, or a hint to the starts of 18-, 25-, 44- and 57- across:" PIDAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/14/2012&g=65&d=A ...
Thank you for the Paul Simon references. I also loved "Only Living Boy in New York". Great puzzle!
I guess I'm inn the minority.  I wouldn't refer to this as "clean and stylish"

Whaddayoumeanism is even more frustrating than 5D. I remember colleagues using that to sidestep answering any question to avoid responsibility. Corporate cubicle farm passive aggression at it's best 😉

Speaking of SVU and fursona, an early season of CSI featured a crime at a Furrie convention. That's the first I've heard of such a subculture (shiver).

And, to yesterday's solver who solves these puzzles high, KAC did you a solid with 53A

Another fun, crunchy solve. Loved FURSONA which I’ve never herd of although I have herd of Furries and PuppyPlay. 
NE corner was a challenge. I don’t smoke anything anymore bit coulda used a TOKE(S) or two

Like our constructor, Kameron Austin Collins, I remember learning the word POLLIWOG as a child, as an alternative to "tadpole", and loving it immediately.  Such a fun word!  It sounds like it was invented by a curious child!

And, having looked up its etymology this morning, I love it even more, learning that the POLL comes from an Old English word for "head" (and is connected to our sense of POLL as in "poll tax" or "straw poll") and WOG comes from the same root as "wiggle".  A wiggling head, which is just what a POLLIWOG looks like!

Of course, I should also admit that I had first put buLLfrOGS in that space, and was very pleased with myself...for a while.

But making mistakes and correcting them is a big part of the fun of a Saturday puzzle.
Thanks for a true Saturday, Kameron Austin Collins!

Oh, and one more puzzle find - very much in the vicinity of dad jokes. A Sunday from February 12, 2017 by Lynn Lempel with the title: "Do the splits." Some theme clue and answer examples (first the one that led me there): "Berate some guy for getting too much sun?" GOOFFONATANGENT "Suggestion to a bored short story writer?" POETRYREADING "Roker's appeal before gastric bypass surgery?" FATALATTRACTION "That villain in comics has sure gotta be sore!" FUMANCHUMUSTACHE And a couple of other theme answers - you'll have to guess the clues: LABORPARTY UNFUNDEDMANDATES Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/12/2017&g=23&d=A ....
FURSONA? I'm getting too old for this.

GOTTA love this fun Saturday  grid-- thank you, Kameron!

Happy Pi Day, everyone! 
3.14  π 🥧 π
We ‘traditionally’ expect our (hopefully) last big snowfall on (or about) March 17th, but it looks like our forecast of “flurries” today has turned into an early Saint Pi trick storm.

Saturday puzzles usually mean several revisits throughout the day, a couple of "check puzzles" and a solve time over an hour. Today I finished in 17:42 over my morning coffee.  Excuse me while I go
buy a lottery ticket.

Hey, ol' pal, ol' bosom buddy, are you a Bae or BFF? Or a roo.

Suitably chewy for a Saturday, NW and SE corners were slow to fill, for me. Enough pop culture, not my bailiwick, that as TUNDRA filled in 41A, I wondered what series was that and who was the filmmaker (23A). TUN-Duh.

I bet no one will say this wasn't a "real" Saturday or a "worthy" Saturday.  I struggled everywhere and finally "solved" it with one cheat: ATTA.  APOSTLE before ACOLYTE for the follower.  WALK before MALL for the promenade.  Are BELTLINES a Thing?  Is BLOTTY a word?  I thought BLOTCHY was the word.  

BOOKTOK (?!) must be a tik tok thing.  Yes?

I had a lot of trouble with PELT for the tanning target, even though I had an absolutely certain "P" and an absolutely certain "T".  (I wanted PECS, but it didn't work with the "T".)

I'm actually much too tired from doing the puzzle to write anything more about it.  Stupendously difficult.
Started easy and then....not so much  = good Saturday puzzle.

Definitely a worthy Saturday. I got stuck in the SW, in the SE, in the middle...

Was so sure 39A was "lope." But almost equally sure that 33D started with BOOK. Those two ideas were at war with each other for a long time.

Almost every guess I had about everything else was either one letter too short or one letter too long. How did the constructor manage that?

Put in the O for BOOKTOK and aargh, “at least one square is…”

Start combing through the puzzle…

Ya know that’s a funny way to spell WHATABOUTYSM, but I learned POLLYWOG when I was younger. 

Problem solved and an alternate spelling was learned.

Can anyone help me with a problem of 3 old puzzles never looking completed even though Check Puzzle finds no errors? I  don't care about the status but I'd like to get them gone from my In Progess list. If any of you have done any of them, can you tell me how they look to you?
Sat Oct 17 2015
Thu Mar 18 2021
Sun May 1 2022
These are excellent puzzles so if anyone cares to try one
SPOILER ALERT




some squares are left blank.

The tech support people tell me to clear cache(which I don't know how to do on my phone's browser app) or use a different platform (which I am also having trouble with because Nytimes wants me to Subscribe and is not giving me the option to put in my current id and password.)
  But in any case I would like to know if this happens to anyone else.

The word “persona” is so rich.  Most of us first run across it in the phrase “Dramatis Personae”, the cast of characters listed at the beginning of a theatrical drama.  But its origin in Latin means “masks” of the drama, and we need to remember that in Classical culture the actors wore masks that defined their “character” (and allowed a single actor to play multiple roles simply by switching masks).

In psychology, the idea of these personas takes hold as the “masks” we show to different people, one for friends, one for work, one for the family.
But where, then, is one’s “true” identity?
That the Latin "persona"/mask lies at the root of the English word “person” makes the question even more salient.  Is there a “true self”?  Or are we just a collection of “masks”?
Many an analyst has been able to make their boat payments by helping their patients work through such questions.

All this is prelude to my morning meditation upon FURSONA, a FURry peRSONA???
I love a good portmanteau word, but really?
At first this seemed to me almost pathological.  
But then I thought, “Why not?”  As the poet says, “I am large.  I contain multitudes.” 
Why shouldn’t some of my personas be furry?

I had "yokels" instead of "yahoos" for a long time. It was a tough puzzle and took me almost an hour, but faster the morning after when I was more alert. I found the puzzle very original, with things I have never seen in a puzzle like "fursona" and "godfearing." It's always nice not to have  oreos and arias.
My love of old movies, occasionally comes in handy. Today, it was Hud, a classic Paul Newman movie. Patricia Neal, shined in her supporting role.

I had a rather BLOTTY solve today. It started with APOSTLE in 3D, and took a long time to straighten out. BREAST PIN? At least I had MOONRAKER as a gimme in the center stack. BTW, "flutters" are BETS in the UK. In the end, a worthy Saturday challenge.
Today I learned that esophagus, fallopian, and INTESTINE all have the same number of letters!

After last week’s incredibly challenging Saturday, I was nervous about this one, especially when my first pass yielded only a handful of confident answers! But this one came together piece by piece, and even though the NE quadrant had me banging my head against the wall by the end, I was surprised and pleased to finish it with no lookups — and eight minutes under my average time! What a heartening way to kick off the weekend. Great puzzle!
Nice to see FURSONA in this one.  Ive heard people use that term enough that it seems like its worked its way well into the modern lexicon.
I guess BRA wasn’t the answer to “bosom buddy” after all…
This was a day of working and frequently reworking the crosses. All worthwhile when a charming answer emerges from the effort. I’m looking at you, FURSONA.

I'm pretty sure I've never seen the movie HUD -- starring Paul Newman, Melvyn Douglas, and Patricia Neal --  but I distinctly remember the parody of this movie in "Mad" magazine.

A quick internet search tells me that the movie came out in May, 1963, and the parody ("Hood!") came out in December, '63.
Wow!  That's quite a while ago!

40A [Some rise by SIN and some by virtue fall." Shak.] For a deeper look into this convoluted play: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Measure_for_Measure And note that the ruling character has different masks and more than one persona.

Felt like a genius typing BULLFROGS in 37A, and so many crosses worked that I fought tooth and nail to keep it in there. Finally realized that WHATABOUTS*M was WHATABOUTISM and revisited it. Fun puzzle.

I was a bit concerned when my first pass left the grid looking strikingly similar to when I started, but my blank stare soon faded. I'm new to NYT crosswords (not a total noob; ex WaPo subscriber), so I'm not sure if my time of 28:02 is respectable or not, although it seems pretty decent to me. One stumbling block was POLLIWOGS, for which I had GOLLIWOGS. Fans of CCR—I'm one—might recognize that word. It was the band's name before they became CCR, a useless piece of trivia that led me astray.
Today I learned that SESAME STREET and SEx in the City have the same number of letters. (All those Emmys for Best Footwear!.)

I don't do passes.  I solve by quadrant.  I don't time myself. I turned off the timer.  I don't need bells and whistles and a pat on then back from a computer program. The more daydreaming and fact chasing I can get out of a crossword puzzle is a win for me.  I am not in a race to finish.  --  The NYT does keep records  of my solve times and I wonder if my deck sitting and bird watching has an effect on solve time averages for everyone.  Anybody?

Took 42% less time than average to solve this one, but I am sure some of the speed I can attribute to my solving partner.

I'm probably not the only one who did this since both answers are apt and have four of the same letters in the exact same position, but had bullfrog before polliwog at 37A.

Fun factoid related to 52A:

When learning body plans in comparative anatomy or embryology, when students first encounter the complete gut (complete in this context means having two ends -- mouth and an anus), they are told that this means humans (because humans have complete guts) are a "tube within a tube." 

Some believe this applies only to worms with tube-like bodies, but it really applies to any organism with a complete gut, including humans, even though the "inner tube" (the gut) is highly convoluted in humans and the outer tube (epidermis) is not very tubelike. Topologically, humans are still a "tube within a tube." This is especially evident during early embryonic development, when gastrulation first forms the gut (by forming the anus, since humans are deuterostomes) and then the mouth forms. The "tube within a tube" structure is very obvious at the point when the mouth is formed. 

FYI, yes, we clams are also a tube within a tube (with some very interesting exceptions), but with an important difference: we are protostomes -- our blastopore forms our mouth, not our anus. That shows up later. I think the situation in humans (and other deuterostomes) is rather... unappetizing. 

This is your comparative anatomy and embryology lesson for the day.

I wanted to say that woks are more for stir frying than deep frying in my neck of the woods, but I started asking myself since when do woods have necks and soon enough I was constructing a puzzle myself, entirely in my mind, a Sunday, 21x21, with a complete theme and many many clever moments and was about to mentally submit it, until I reached the very last square and couldn't make it wok.

I found this one tough but satisfying. Age-wise, I’m too young for MOONRAKER, and too old (I guess) for FURSONA, but that’s what I love about crosswords: you always learn something new. As a history teacher, I always love the historical references, like WHIG. If they refer to something we are learning in the class I teach, I will even include a screenshot in my PowerPoint for that week, as an example of cultural literacy. (My most recent one was PLEBS while we studied Ancient Rome, and SPARTA during our study of Greece. The clue even mentioned the Peloponnesian War!)

Speaking of cultural literacy, that’s one of the great things about crosswords: it increases it! I understand complaining when a piece of trivia seems too arcane or vapid, but the flip side is, you learned something about the society you live in! I will never be a furry, for example, but FURSONA is clever and gave me a chuckle this morning.
Definitely some things I’ve never heard of before(fursona, booktok) but all in all a representative Saturday.
This one was a lot of fun

For me, it would have to be PURRSONA.

I miss my cats. Lately, I have been watching some cat videos, especially cats behaving badly ones.

If I was rich, I'd build a screened-in catio in my yard!

Any nice cat stories?
Generational pop culture frustration seems to be the Comments theme of the day but I've found that you can oftentimes in-fur the answer from a well-worded clue.
Good week for polycephaly (sort of)--Here's singer/songwriter Annie Ross, jivin' with her Brain A: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5TmUmrIoMi8&list=RD5TmUmrIoMi8&start_radio=1

Just wanted to chime in that this was a really nice puzzle. Loved the construction, the cluing, the fresh multigenerational references (if you just learned FURSONA, congrats, a lot of people under 40 just learned HUD), the misdirection. A great way to start my Saturday.

I always love me a Kam Collins crossword, ever since his NYT debut when he chimed in on the comments, never fails to make it a chase and a treasure hunt. Great grid today is supported by a hilarious TWOHEADED column photograph, which I'll nitpick by suggesting it's a wallaby instead of a kangaroo. No way to tell its size, but a wallaby (like Joseph) has a coat of many colours. Could even be a pademelon! Once again, KamC made me glad I'm among the generations that enjoyed SESAME_STREET, which deserved every Emmy, and even made a place for Oscar. Plenty celebs were happy to BELT_LINES on that show: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wqJjrtDFGpI Admitting I was afraid 34A clue Bosom buddy might turn out to be BRA, but I spose 1A BRAY was close enough. Appreciated HEDY Lamarr (Hungarian), and happy we didn't die INTESTate. Me too about POLLIWOGS, Mr Collins. Give the man ARRAYS!!

I want to thank Ms. Lamarr for convincing me that Pitt was a WHIG and not a TORY which would have sent in the wrong way for quite a while.

Very enjoyable puzzle.
I thought it was a good fill overall, even if FURSONA was an odious portmanteau. In any case, 200. Some have much longer streaks, but this is a good one for me.
Once I switched models to the Toyota TUNDRA instead of Tacoma, I was able to finish the solve. Tough one
I have a new favorite word: fursona.

It's not often I finish a Saturday puzzle without googling at least a few clues, and thought this one would be no different. After my first pass I only had five or six words, only three or four of which I was sure of. But subsequent passes kept filling in more with a few educated (?) guesses from the crosses, and it slowly fell into place without any web searches.

All in all a very satisfying half hour solve, well ahead of my average.

Add me to the list who had buLLfrOGS for too long ...

WHATABOUTISM -- another very topical entry.

Good, challenging Saturday.

⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️

I thought I knew what could croak...
Tadpoles -- oops too short
HornToads -- worked for awhile, but then crosses didn't fit
Oh!  I had the LL, so it must be bullfrogs.
Then more crosses didn't work.
Finally!  POLLIWOGS!!

Usually with a Saturday puzzle I have no idea.  For this one there were too many good possiblilties.

Great Puzzle.  I got it all with only 2 look-ups.  Not bad for me on Saturday.
1.  I should have known PAULSIMON wrote The Boxer, but I wasn't thinking music.  Simon and Garfunkle were my favorites in high school and college.  
2.  I should learn the Indian cooking terms.  Past naan and roti I'm stuck.  ATTA is the Asian OREO??
BREASTPINS and TBA ... alas. CUFFLINKS to start, I'd tell you how I got from one to another, but that's TBD ... as most people say.

This one was a lot of fun. It made me work a lot harder than yesterday as well. I was another buLLfrOG person for a long time and then I decided that POLLyWOG needed a y. 

It took me way too long to come up with Moonraker because I was a big Bond fan when it came out although that one was not a favorite. Surprisingly I remembered S&G did The Boxer and happily I guessed correctly which one wrote it.

FURSONA was, very surprisingly, not the last word I completed but it was close. All in all a well spent 23 minutes.
Little easier than yesterday for me. A fine puzzle but I would have swapped today and Friday for difficulty.

INTESTINES sent me down this  chain of thought.  If you ignore the nasal passages, the human body is topologically a torus. One of the common exemplars of tori is the inner tube. Around and around we go.

Those of you who don't know Simon & Garfunkel's The Boxer don't realize what great poetry you're missing out on. A beautiful paean to the grit of America's working-class poor. Paul Simon is an amazing songwriter. https://youtu.be/l3LFML_pxlY?si=nApZp8VBy5jMOXTj
I'm ashamed to admit how long I stuck with PODHEARING for 8 Down.

@logical

No. The answer is a collective noun. It can refer to many examples of the same thing. There is no rule that clue and answer must agree grammatically in number, only that the sense of the clue matches that of the answer.

@Steve L
This is a close call for me. I suppose you could say “He used WHATABOUTISM to get his argument across”—“He used some deflections…” etc. But Merriam Webster does show examples with “whataboutisms” plural to indicate more than one of the deflections. Personally, I think it would be better clued in the singular, and you might say it was a misdirection but you don’t usually misdirect on an arcane knowledge clue. Loved the entry, it didn’t bother me too much, and don’t want to nitpick too much about it; but I’d lean towards voting with the OP if it were my choice.

No, it should not be plural. 
(1) The answer is a collective noun (as Steve notes). 
(2) There is only one "argument" in the clue (as SP misses?).

@Barry Ancona
As for 2), “argument” isn’t the subject, if that were the point they would say “an argument using some deflections…” Even “using some deflections in an argument…” would have beenn cleaner to me. I’m going more with anonymous’s interpretation that it was a deliberate misdirection and that a WHATABOUTISM could be a collection of deflections in a single argument.

Compared to an average Saturday from 10+ years ago? Very easy. But regardless, this puzzle wasn’t insultingly easy, and I doubt you’ll hear much complaining about it.

Truth be told, the people who tend to complain about how the puzzles have gotten too damn easy (myself included) would much rather *not* be having that conversation, and from my perspective, the volume of such complaints has only risen above a murmur in some pretty egregious cases (e.g. the infamous Valentine’s Day cakewalk of 2026).

Btw, I certainly needed help from multiple crosses to get MOONRAKER and PAULSIMON (and SESAMESTREET, actually). But there was enough connectivity throughout the entire grid to prevent any one answer from becoming a bottleneck (or being a stranded peninsula), and to enable incrementally building up the longer answers.

Fine.

I guess you could include the Wall Street Journal if all you care about is money, that the Dow is over 50000 dollars.

And don't think no one noticed your MINNESOTANICE Pam Bondi diss.

@Francis
Since the Dow has become a hot topic lately, it’s useful to understand that it’s not a dollar figure.  The Dow index value is the sum of the stock prices of its 30 component companies divided by a “divisor” that adjusts for events like stock splits that affect share prices.

Some great songs then.  Ten years later Herman Hupfield wrote "As Time Goes By," and Dooley Wilson made it famous ten years after that in "Casablanca."  I keep coming back to it, because it pretty much says it all.

As Time Goes By

You must remember this
A kiss is just a kiss
A sigh is just a sigh
The fundamental things apply

They still say, "I love you"
On that you can rely

Woman needs man, and man must have his mate
That no one can deny
It's still the same old story
A fight for love and glory
A case of do-or-die
The world will always welcome lovers
As time goes by

@Francis Here's a joyful Paul Simon video - Late in the Evening https://tinyurl.com/y7cj3trw There's another audio-only "Official Video" with his album cover photo from 1976. Can it be fifty years?

@Francis I saw Paul Simon in concert in 1991, and it was an amazing experience. The tour was for Rhythm of the Saints but it was soon after Graceland (one of my favorite albums of all time) and he played a lot of those songs. I can still hear the crowd singing “Call … me … Al” when Simon cued us. A magical night with a dear friend.

(Years later I saw Art Garfunkel in concert. His voice was lovely and we especially enjoyed the many Paul Simon songs, but let’s just say I gained new insight about why Simon stopped working with him.)

@Francis
I've saw Paul Simon in Hyde Park in London, 2017, and in wonderful voice.  Fabulous concert. Three generations of families on the grass in the sun.

@Francis

I have a special bittersweet connection to The Boxer.  In the wee hours of the morning my parents had bundled me into the car because we had to drive from NJ to Buffalo because they had gotten news that my grandmother had been hit by a car while waiting for a bus earlier that evening. While lying curled up in the back seat, groggy and half asleep, and fearful and sick at heart at what we were going to find when we got to the hospital, sure that my grandmother was going to die, I heard The Boxer, which had just been released for air play, for the first time.Not surprisingly the song was seared into my brain.  The attitude of stoic defiance in the song proved prophetic.  Despite breaking both hips, my grandmother fought through the pain and after many, many months of recuperation and physical therapy learned to walk again.  I can’t hear the song without thinking of her fierce will to live and of the jumble of emotions I felt on that car ride.

@Marshall Walthew
That's an incredible story! Thanks. 🙏

@John @Barry Ancona
  There are recipes for deep-frying in a wok. And brings back happy memories of making tempura fish in a wok  during a long ago family vacation on Okracoke Island when my husband, mother and brother-in-law had gone charter fishing and come back with 20 plus fish. We have pix of the 4 to 6 year olds covered in flour as they helped with the prep.

@Barry Ancona It isn’t, actually. A lot of people deep fry in woks. They’re huge, deep vessels, so they can hold enough oil to deep fry things like chicken and fries. You’re not gonna be frying turkeys in there, though!

@Barry Ancona

The hyphen says the action is deep frying, as in making french fries. It wouldn't be there to fit your wordplay theory. I'm wondering if this is an editing error.

@JD Gold absolutely spot on. The best puzzles are word play and clever themes.

@JD Gold
Me too. I'm happy to learn POLLIWOG and BREAST PIN (I think) not cars from 1999. I haven't seen WHIG since I was in history class aged 13. That's along time ago.

JD Gold, Are you quite sure "pop culture and hobbies" weren't there in the old days, or are you just not keeping up with things as much as you used to? https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/1996/03/16

@Barry Ancona Of course they were, in proportion and usually with much more universality. But these types of fills that require you to have seen a movie that flopped at the box office, or participate in an activity using a specific sort of twee, age-specific argot were definitely not par for the course.

You don't have to *be* a furry to know FURSONA; just (continue to) read The New York Times.

There has always been "twee, age-specific argot" in the Crossword; when it was your age, it didn't bother you.

@JD Gold, I agree with you, for the most part. But I just did a couple Saturdays from 1995 in the last two nights, courtesy of a suggestion here, and I would say at least a third of those puzzles were then-contemporary trivia. One was Elizabeth Taylor, which was easily gettable (from the crosses, not the 1960s movie clue), but several others were about flash-in-the-pan 1970s TV shows and lesser politicians. 

Like you, I do very much prefer the general knowledge wordplay clues. But I don’t mind learning something new.

@JD Gold the idea that the constructors and editors of these puzzles are “lazy” is wild to me. The amount of thought and smarts they must put into these blows my mind! I can hand on heart say I’ve never heard of a fursona - but it slowly came into view as a possible answer from the crosses and my awareness of the words “fur” and “persona”. Similarly I know nothing of Toyota models or of a movie about someone called Hud (oh how confidently wrote in Hur 😅). But that’s part of the joy for me! Still, if it’s not for you it’s not for you. There’s probably some I’ve grinded teeth over that you’ve sailed through with glee.

@JD Gold
This was an elaborate test of one's age and extent of keeping up with the times. 
If you easily got "Moonraker" but not "Fursona" then you're over thirty. If you managed it the other way around, you're under 30. 
Puzzle constructor: I thought it was hip.
Ya feel?

I hit the same problem a couple of months ago.  I'm too cheap to buy a new iPad.  Planned obsolescense makes me furious!!  Why should Apple force me to spend hundreds of dollars for something I only need for 1 app???

But, there is a good work-around.
Go to the NYT Apps and you can play in Games, at the bottom.  Until the puzzle for the next day hits.  For me that is 7pm and I usually do the puzzle after dinner...
Or nytimes.com/crosswords gets you there, and if the new puzzle is out you can go to the Archives at the bottom and do the one for today.  

Get Wordplay by going to the Sections tab at the bottom of NYTimes App and search on WORDPLAY..

I reproduce FYI the letter written (in my head) to the NYT years ago but never published.  That's because I never sent it in.   I think I should have, though.   It was written when the Times went to 6 sections on Tuesday or something very much like that:

Dear New York Times:

I love you very much.  You are an important part of my life.  You cannot, however, be my ENTIRE life.  

Love, Nancy (Last name followed).

Nancy,
Of course you can be forgiven, but what's with the recitation of current events? FURSONA has been around for almost 30 years. For how long have you been getting too old for this? In any case, I hope you're not getting too old to learn a new word after entering it from crosses.

@RozzieGrandma, are you using the Safari browser on your iPhone? If so, open Settings. Scroll down to Safari. Select “clear history [all history]” This will log you out of any sites you are currently logged into on Safari, and you will need to log in again next time you visit each site. To fix the login issue on the New York Times website: Go to NYTimes.com on Safari. Select “Account” at the top right and try to log in. If it doesn’t work, select “Forgot password.” It will probably send you an email with a link to reset your password, or it might send you a text message, depending on how you have it set up. If you use Chrome instead of Safari, here is a link with instructions: https://www.wikihow.com/Clear-Your-Browser's-Cache-on-an-iPhone Once you’ve done all this, go back to those dates in the Crosswords archive. You should see that the puzzles are no longer downloaded. Click on the date to freshly download each of those puzzles. Hopefully, they show as complete now, but if not, try deleting one letter and re-entering it. See if you get the completion message. If not, submit a help request here: https://help.nytimes.com/360052406391-The-New-York-Times-Crossword-Puzzle

@Captain Quahog Fun fact: "factoid" originally means something without evidence that is generally believed to be true only because of repetition, especially in the press. It is now commonly used to mean its near opposite—an interesting minor fact—only because of repetition, especially in the press. Use it as you prefer, but not in ignorance.
